basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: al-Raḥmān — “The All-Merciful” — is the great litany of gratitude: a single Name opens the sūra, and from it flows everything — the teaching of the Qurʾān, the creation of man, speech, the heavenly bodies, the seas, the gardens. Thirty-one times the refrain returns: “So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?” — addressed to humans and jinn. Qushayrī opens on the mystery of prior teaching: He taught the Qurʾān to the spirits before lodging them in bodies, and makes mercy the source of all: it is by His mercy that they reached the Qurʾān, not by the Qurʾān that they reach His mercy. The pivot-verse — “all that is on earth is perishable, and the Face of your Lord abides” — tips the sūra from the created gift to the sole Abiding, in His Majesty (jalāl) and Munificence (ikrām). It culminates on the gardens promised to him who feared the station of his Lord, and on the overwhelming word: “is the reward of good anything but good?”

versets.trad: The All-Merciful,
versets.commentaire: The sūra opens on the single Name al-Raḥmān, the All-Merciful. Qushayrī: the All-Merciful whom the believers have known and the deniers have denied, it is He who taught the Qurʾān. The Merciful who took them into mercy, preserved them from associating partners, honoured them with faith, and made them hold to the word of godfearing — it is He who instructed them in the Qurʾān. All creation will flow from this Name: mercy is the spring from which teaching gushes forth.

mots.racine: ر ح م

versets.trad: He made the Qurʾān known;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God reserved to Himself the teaching of the Qurʾān to His servants. And he reads in it a mystery of priority: He taught the Qurʾān to the spirits before lodging them in bodies, without intermediary — like the love that precedes the knowledge of love: “her love came to me before I knew love; it found my heart empty and settled in it.” He adds: it is by His mercy that He taught them the Qurʾān, and by His mercy that they reached the Qurʾān — not by reciting the Qurʾān that they reach His mercy.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ق ر أ
mots.def: ʿallama: to teach; al-Qurʾān: the Recitation.

versets.trad: He created man;
versets.commentaire: The order is telling: God mentions the teaching of the Qurʾān before the creation of man. Qushayrī sees in it a mark of love: the grace of the Word precedes in dignity the shaping of the being. Man (al-insān) is created only to receive what was destined for him from eternity — knowledge.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / أ ن س
mots.def: khalaqa: to create; insān: the human being.

versets.trad: He taught him to express himself.
versets.commentaire: The bayān — clear utterance — distinguishes man from every animal. Qushayrī: He taught each people its language; the bayān is that by which meanings are made manifest. But he reveals its spiritual sense: the bayān of the people of faith and gnosis is their knowledge of how to address their Master; and the servants' address to the Real differs: some speak to Him with their tongue, some with their breath, some with their tears — “the young man's tears translate what he feels, and his yearnings reveal what he conceals.”

mots.racine: ع ل م / ب ي ن

versets.trad: The sun and the moon follow a reckoning.
themes: the order of the heavenly bodies
versets.commentaire: The sun and the moon run by a precise reckoning (ḥusbān). Qushayrī transposes: likewise the suns of gnosis and the moons of the sciences, in their rising at the zenith of hearts and secrets, have in God's wisdom a known reckoning, which He makes run according to what the decree has gone before. The order of the visible heaven is the image of the hidden order by which light rises in hearts.

mots.racine: ش م س / ق م ر
ح س ب

versets.trad: The herbs and the trees prostrate.
versets.commentaire: The stemless herb (najm) and the stemmed tree (shajar) prostrate. Qushayrī: the rising stars and the firm trees prostrate — a prostration of witness, attesting the Maker's existence and His right to majesty. All nature is in the posture of worship; only forgetful man stands upright in denial.

mots.racine: ن ج م / ش ج ر
س ج د

versets.trad: The heaven He raised, and He set the balance
versets.commentaire: God raised the heaven and set the balance (mīzān). Qushayrī: He raised the heaven, built it in perfection and firmness, set the stars coursing through it; and He created the balance among men that they might observe equity in their dealings. The balance is justice (al-ʿadl). The same God who orders the heavens orders the dealings of men: equity is a cosmic law.

mots.racine: س م و / ر ف ع
و ض ع / و ز ن

versets.trad: that you may not transgress in the weighing.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī widens the balance to every domain: observe justice in all things, in the rights of men and the rights of God. In deeds, sincerity (ikhlāṣ) is weighed; in states, truthfulness (ṣidq); in breaths, the realities, the equality of outer and inner, the abandonment of flattery, cunning, the subtleties of hidden idolatry and the recesses of hypocrisy.

mots.racine: ط غ ي / و ز ن
mots.sens: that you not defraud in the balance

versets.ar: وَأَقِيمُوا الْوَزْنَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا الْمِيزَانَ
versets.tr: wa-aqīmū l-wazna bi-l-qisṭi wa-lā tukhsirū l-mīzāna
versets.trad: So establish the weighing with justice and do not skimp the balance.
versets.commentaire: Equity (qisṭ) is commanded as an act of worship. Qushayrī: establish the weighing by the very measure you would wish used for you, without skimping anything. To skimp the balance is to do to another what one would not bear for oneself: outward justice is the mirror of inward uprightness.

mots.racine: ق و م / و ز ن / ق س ط
خ س ر / و ز ن
mots.def: akhsara: to cause loss, to skimp.

versets.trad: The earth He laid out for the creatures.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He created the earth and made it a cradle and dwelling for the creatures; He laid it upon the water, spread out its regions, made trees and flowers grow in it, rivers flow, darkened its night and lit its day. The earth is no accident but a furnished gift: every detail of the habitable world is an attention of the Creator for the living (anām).

mots.racine: أ ر ض / و ض ع / أ ن م
mots.sens: the earth, He laid it for the creatures

versets.trad: In it are fruits and palm-trees with sheaths,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī details the profusion: fruits diverse in colours, savours, scents, and virtues; and the palm with its spathes (akmām) that envelop and protect. Each sheath is a care: nature itself protects what it offers — an image of the divine solicitude that ripens its gifts under cover before giving them.

mots.racine: ف ك ه
ن خ ل / ك م م

versets.trad: grain in the husk and fragrant herbs.
versets.commentaire: The husked grain (ʿaṣf) and the fragrant herb (rayḥān). Qushayrī recalls that the Arabs called rizq (sustenance) “God's rayḥān”: He reminded them of the immensity of His grace by what He created of these things from which they profit — foods, scents, and the rest. From the grain that feeds to the fragrance that delights, the favour nourishes the body and gladdens the soul.

mots.racine: ح ب ب / ع ص ف
ر و ح

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: He created man from clay like pottery;
versets.commentaire: Man is drawn from sounding clay (ṣalṣāl) dried like pottery. Qushayrī: He reminded Adam of his origin and reminded us of ours, that we might not grow proud of our states; He made him know his measure that he might not exceed his rank. And he notes the hidden grace: He raised him to his rank after creating him from clay — the lowliness of the origin makes the loftiness of the gift blaze out.

mots.racine: ص ل ص ل / ف خ ر
mots.def: ṣalṣāl: dry resonant clay; fakhkhār: pottery.

versets.ar: وَخَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِن مَّارِجٍ مِّن نَّارٍ
versets.tr: wa-khalaqa l-jānna min mārijin min nārin
versets.trad: He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire.
versets.commentaire: The jinn is created from mārij — the pure flame mingled with fire, without smoke. Two opposite natures, clay and flame, come from one creating hand. Qushayrī stresses that the diversity of origins proclaims the Creator's oneness: it is the same Power that kneads the earth and kindles the fire.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ج ن ن
mots.def: jānn: the jinn, beings of fire.

mots.racine: م ر ج / ن و ر

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: Lord of the two easts and Lord of the two wests.
themes: the two orients, the two wests
versets.commentaire: The two easts: that of summer and that of winter, likewise the wests. Qushayrī: the favour in it is that they run by a single order, until the creatures take full profit from them. And he reads its inner sense: the east of the heart and its west, its points of dawn and decline, are the lights and the inner sights the sūra has already spoken of.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ش ر ق
غ ر ب

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: He let the two seas flow, they meet;
versets.commentaire: The two seas meet without mingling. Qushayrī gives the spiritual key: He created in hearts two seas: the sea of fear (khawf) and the sea of hope (rajāʾ); also: contraction (qabḍ) and expansion (basṭ); also: awe (hayba) and intimacy (uns). From them come the pearls and jewels — the pure states and the successive subtleties.

mots.racine: م ر ج / ب ح ر
mots.sens: He let the two seas flow
mots.def: maraja: to let flow together; baḥrayn: two seas.

mots.racine: ل ق ي

versets.trad: between them is a barrier they do not overpass.
versets.commentaire: The barzakh is the unseen barrier that keeps the two seas from merging. Qushayrī, in the inner reading, identifies the two seas with the soul (nafs) and the heart (qalb): the heart is the sweet sea, the soul the salt sea. From the sea of the heart comes every precious jewel and every subtle state; from the soul, every blameworthy trait. Between the two, a barrier: the Real preserves one from the other, and neither overpasses the other.

mots.racine: ب ر ز خ
ب غ ي

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: From the two come pearls and coral.
versets.commentaire: From the meeting of the two seas come pearls (luʾluʾ) and coral (marjān). Qushayrī: from the sea of the heart — that of fear and hope, of contraction and expansion — surge the jewels of the spiritual states. What is precious is not born of stagnant water but of the living tension between two poles: fear and hope, contraction and dilation.

mots.racine: خ ر ج / ل أ ل أ
م ر ج ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: وَلَهُ الْجَوَارِ الْمُنشَآتُ فِي الْبَحْرِ كَالْأَعْلَامِ
versets.tr: wa-lahu l-jawāri l-munshaʾātu fī l-baḥri ka-l-aʿlāmi
versets.trad: His are the ships that rise upon the sea like mountains.
versets.commentaire: The ships (jawārī) raised upon the sea, high as mountains (aʿlām), belong to God. The favour is not only the sea but the power to cross it: God gives both the fearsome element and the means to master it. All that bears man across the abyss is yet a gift.

mots.racine: ج ر ي / ن ش أ
mots.def: jawārī: ships (lit. runners); munshaʾāt: built up.

mots.racine: ع ل م

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: All that is upon it passes away,
versets.commentaire: The pivot-verse of the sūra: all that is on earth is perishable (fānin). Qushayrī: all that is on the face of the earth is under the law of evanescence. This is the great lesson of impermanence: nothing created lasts, and the heart that clings to the passing clings to sand that runs away.

mots.racine: ك ل ل
ف ن ي

versets.ar: وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ
versets.tr: wa-yabqā wajhu rabbika dhū l-jalāli wa-l-ikrāmi
versets.trad: but the Face of your Lord abides, full of Majesty and Munificence.
versets.commentaire: To the perishable answers the Abiding: wa-yabqā wajhu rabbik. Qushayrī: in the abiding of the Face is the abiding of the Essence, for the attribute does not subsist by itself. And in His permanence, glory be to Him, is a compensation for every loss, and a consolation for the believers for all the trials that strike them and the gifts that escape them. The Face (wajh) that abides in Majesty (jalāl) and Munificence (ikrām) is the only support that never collapses: whoever clings to it loses nothing.

mots.racine: ب ق ي / و ج ه
mots.sens: the Face of your Lord abides

mots.racine: ج ل ل / ك ر م

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: يَسْأَلُهُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۚ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ
versets.tr: yasʾaluhu man fī s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi kulla yawmin huwa fī shaʾnin
versets.trad: All in the heavens and the earth ask of Him. Every day He is at some work.
versets.commentaire: All creation ceaselessly asks, and every day He is at some work (kulla yawmin huwa fī shaʾn). Qushayrī: of His work is to forgive a sin, to veil a flaw, to dispel an anguish, to gladden a heart, to distance one servant and draw another near. At every instant He has for His servants a new favour, and a secret between Him and His servant, far from all eyes. He refutes the Jews who claimed God rested: were He to deprive the universe of His keeping for an instant, all would collapse.

mots.racine: س أ ل
mots.sens: all in heavens and earth ask of Him

mots.racine: ش أ ن
mots.sens: every day He is at some work

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: Soon We shall attend to you, O two burdened ones!
versets.commentaire: “We shall attend to you” (sa-nafrughu lakum) does not mean God is until then busy elsewhere — Qushayrī rejects this: it means: We shall turn to your judgment on the Day of Resurrection. The two burdens (thaqalān) are humans and jinn, so named because they weigh upon the earth. The reckoning will come: none escapes the account.

mots.racine: ف ر غ
mots.def: faragha: to free oneself, to attend to.

mots.racine: ث ق ل

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: يَا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَن تَنفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطَارِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانفُذُوا ۚ لَا تَنفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطَانٍ
versets.tr: yā maʿshara l-jinni wa-l-insi ini staṭaʿtum an tanfudhū min aqṭāri s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi fa-nfudhū lā tanfudhūna illā bi-sulṭānin
versets.trad: O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass beyond the regions of the heavens and the earth, then pass! But you will not pass save with an authority...
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: if you can come out of His Kingdom, come out! But you will reach no place without My authority and My dominion being there; you will pierce no region without there being against you a proof. There is no flight outside the divine Kingdom: wherever the creature goes, it remains under the Sovereignty that envelops it on all sides.

mots.racine: ط و ع / ن ف ذ
mots.def: nafadha: to pierce, pass through, exit.

mots.racine: ق ط ر
mots.sens: from the regions of the heavens and earth

mots.racine: س ل ط
mots.sens: you will not pass save with authority

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُمَا شُوَاظٌ مِّن نَّارٍ وَنُحَاسٌ فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ
versets.tr: yursalu ʿalaykumā shuwāẓun min nārin wa-nuḥāsun fa-lā tantaṣirāni
versets.trad: Against you will be sent flames of fire and molten brass, and you will not be helped.
versets.commentaire: Against the one who would flee the Kingdom, jets of flame (shuwāẓ) and molten brass (nuḥās), and no help possible. Qushayrī: fa-lā tantaṣirān — you will be able neither to avenge yourselves nor to defend yourselves. The creature that thinks itself autonomous discovers its absolute impotence: outside God there is neither way out nor defender.

mots.racine: ش و ظ / ن و ر
ن ح س
ن ص ر
mots.def: intaṣara: to defend or avenge oneself.

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: فَإِذَا انشَقَّتِ السَّمَاءُ فَكَانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَالدِّهَانِ
versets.tr: fa-idhā nshaqqati s-samāʾu fa-kānat wardatan ka-d-dihāni
versets.trad: When the heaven splits and becomes rose-red like tanned leather...
versets.commentaire: On the Last Day the heaven splits and reddens like a rose (warda), like reddened oil. Qushayrī: just as the rose changes colour, turning yellow in spring, then red when it opens, then to an ashen hue — so the heaven will tint itself from state to state on the Day of Resurrection. The firmness of the firmament yields: what seemed unchangeable comes undone.

mots.racine: ش ق ق
و ر د / د ه ن
mots.def: warda: rose; dihān: oil, dyed leather.

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْأَلُ عَن ذَنبِهِ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَانٌّ
versets.tr: fa-yawmaʾidhin lā yusʾalu ʿan dhanbihi insun wa-lā jānnun
versets.trad: That Day no human nor jinn will be questioned about his sin.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reconciles this verse with those that announce the questioning: at certain moments of the Resurrection — this Day is long — they will not be questioned, and at others they will be. And he adds: since they bear marks — black face and bluish eyes for the deniers, white face for the believers — the angels need not ask: who are you? for they recognize each by his sign (sīmā).

mots.racine: س أ ل / ذ ن ب
mots.sens: he is not questioned about his sin
mots.def: suʾila: to be questioned; dhanb: sin.

mots.racine: أ ن س / ج ن ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيمَاهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّوَاصِي وَالْأَقْدَامِ
versets.tr: yuʿrafu l-mujrimūna bi-sīmāhum fa-yuʾkhadhu bi-n-nawāṣī wa-l-aqdāmi
versets.trad: The guilty will be known by their marks, and seized by the forelocks and the feet.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the believers will be marked with whiteness on the brow and limbs, the deniers will have black faces and bluish eyes; the angels will recognize them, seize them by the forelock, drag them now by it now by the feet, then cast them into the Fire. The face betrays the heart's state: what the soul was inwardly, the Day makes visible.

mots.racine: ع ر ف / و س م
mots.sens: the criminals are known by their mark

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ن ص ي / ق د م

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: هَٰذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ
versets.tr: hādhihi jahannamu llatī yukadhdhibu bihā l-mujrimūna
versets.trad: This is Hell, which the guilty used to deny!
versets.commentaire: “This is Hell that you used to deny!” Qushayrī: it will be said to them face to face. What they denied in mockery rises before them as an inescapable reality. Denial does not abolish the real; it only delays the encounter, which will be all the more crushing.

mots.racine: ج ه ن م
ك ذ ب / ج ر م

versets.trad: They will circle between it and a boiling water.
versets.commentaire: The guilty circle (yaṭūfūn) between the fire and boiling water at its peak (ḥamīm ān). No respite: from flame to scalding water, the punishment alternates without let. Qushayrī recalls that ḥamīm is water heated to the extreme — image of a torment that leaves no interval of rest.

mots.racine: ط و ف
ح م م / أ ن ي
mots.sens: and a water at the peak of boiling
mots.def: ḥamīm: boiling water; ān: reached its extreme.

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: But for him who feared the station of his Lord, there are two gardens...
versets.commentaire: At the sūra's turning: to reverential fear answer two gardens. Qushayrī: for him who feared the nearness of his Lord and His gaze upon him; for him who dreaded standing tomorrow before God — two gardens. He offers a reading: two real gardens — one anticipated in this world, the sweetness of obedience and the repose of the moment; the other deferred in the Hereafter, the garden of reward. Fear (khawf) is no sadness but the seed of a twofold felicity.

mots.racine: خ و ف / ق و م
mots.sens: for him who feared his Lord's station
mots.def: khāfa: to fear; maqām: station, standing-place.

mots.racine: ج ن ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: two gardens of spreading branches...
versets.commentaire: The two gardens are “of spreading branches” (afnān). Qushayrī: gardens of every colour and every kind that the soul and the eye desire. The luxuriance of the boughs figures the richness of the gifts reserved for him who feared his Lord: fear here below blossoms there above into abundance.

mots.racine: ف ن ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: in which two springs flow...
versets.commentaire: Two springs flow in them. Qushayrī: two springs will flow tomorrow for him who has, today, two springs flowing with tears. The rivers of Paradise are the prolongation of the tears shed in fear: what flows from the heart here is transmuted into living waters there.

mots.racine: ع ي ن / ج ر ي
mots.def: ʿaynān: two springs; jarā: to flow.

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: in which of every fruit there are two kinds.
versets.commentaire: Of every fruit, two kinds (zawjān). Qushayrī: two varieties and two sorts, like the fresh and the dried. Abundance is not monotonous: the very diversity is a gift, where each savour is declined to fulfil every desire.

mots.racine: ف ك ه / ز و ج

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَىٰ فُرُشٍ بَطَائِنُهَا مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ ۚ وَجَنَى الْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ
versets.tr: muttakiʾīna ʿalā furushin baṭāʾinuhā min istabraqin wa-janā l-jannatayni dānin
versets.trad: Reclining on couches lined with brocade, the fruit of the two gardens within reach.
themes: repose and fruit near at hand
versets.commentaire: The linings of the couches are of thick brocade (istabraq) — Qushayrī: if the lining is of brocade, what of the face? But God addresses men according to their understanding: there is nothing in Paradise resembling what is here below; the address is only to the measure of their faculties. And the fruit is within reach (dānin): it descends toward their mouths with no effort at all.

mots.racine: ف ر ش / ب ط ن / ب ر ق
ج ن ي / د ن و
mots.sens: the fruit of the two gardens within reach

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: فِيهِنَّ قَاصِرَاتُ الطَّرْفِ لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَانٌّ
versets.tr: fīhinna qāṣirātu ṭ-ṭarfi lam yaṭmithhunna insun qablahum wa-lā jānnun
versets.trad: In them are maidens of modest gaze, untouched before them by human or jinn.
versets.commentaire: The Paradise spouses lower their gaze (qāṣirāt al-ṭarf) upon their husbands alone. Qushayrī draws a lesson of adab for the servant: if the spouses lower their gaze from other than their husband, all the more does it befit the servant, when he hopes for the encounter with his Lord, to lower his gaze and turn it from all that is forbidden — nay, from everything, until he meets Him. And he evokes those who, by elevation, do not even look at them: among the saints, some will not look at them, though it be permitted, by freedom from desires and loftiness of aspiration.

mots.racine: ق ص ر / ط ر ف
mots.def: qāṣirāt al-ṭarf: who hold back their gaze.

mots.ar: لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَانٌّ
mots.tr: lam yaṭmithhunna insun qablahum wa-lā jānn
mots.racine: ط م ث
mots.sens: untouched by human or jinn before

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: As though they were rubies and coral.
versets.commentaire: They are likened to the ruby (yāqūt) and coral (marjān). Qushayrī: in the limpidity of the ruby and the colour of the coral. The translucent purity of the gem figures the transparency of a being without stain: the beauty of Paradise is first of all purity.

mots.racine: ي ق ت / م ر ج ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: Is the reward of good anything but good?
versets.commentaire: A luminous verse: is the reward of excellence anything but excellence? Qushayrī unfolds its nested senses: the first iḥsān is from God, the second from the servant: the reward of one to whom We do good by support is that he serve Us. Or: the first from the servant, the second from God: the reward of one who serves by obedience is to be received and rewarded. Or both from God: the reward of one to whom We did good at the beginning is to do good to him at the end. And the highest: the reward of one who distanced himself from his soul is that We draw him near; of one who passed away to himself, that he subsist by Us.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ح س ن
mots.sens: is the reward of good but good?

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: And besides these two, two other gardens...
versets.commentaire: Beyond the first two gardens, two more still. Qushayrī clarifies: He does not mean “inferior in merit,” but two gardens in addition to the two. The divine generosity does not run dry: felicity succeeds felicity, and gift calls forth gift.

mots.racine: د و ن / ج ن ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: two dark-green gardens...
versets.commentaire: The two gardens are “dark green” (mudhāmmatān), so deep in verdure they verge on black. Qushayrī: green with a greenness inclining to black. The intensity of colour speaks the intensity of life: a green so deep it becomes a luminous darkness.

mots.racine: د ه م

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: in which two gushing springs burst forth.
versets.commentaire: Two gushing springs (naḍḍākhatān). The verb naḍkh denotes the bubbling burst of water. Where the first gardens had springs “that flow” (verse 50), these have springs that gush with force: a nuance of overflowing abundance.

mots.racine: ع ي ن / ن ض خ

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: In them are fruit, palm-trees, and pomegranates.
versets.commentaire: Fruit, palms, and pomegranates. Qushayrī notes the names' resemblance to the things of this world, but recalls that the names resemble, not the essences: the Qurʾān names the bliss of Paradise with the words of this world, to the measure of our understanding, while the reality surpasses every image.

mots.racine: ف ك ه / ن خ ل / ر م ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: In them are maidens good and beautiful,
versets.commentaire: The Paradise spouses are “good and beautiful” (khayrāt ḥisān). Qushayrī: houris of fair character and fair face. Beauty there is not severed from goodness: the fair exterior proceeds from an inner excellence — the face mirrors the soul.

mots.racine: خ ي ر / ح س ن
mots.sens: in them, good and beautiful ones

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.trad: Houris secluded in the tents...
versets.commentaire: The houris are “secluded in the tents” (maqṣūrāt fī l-khiyām). Qushayrī transposes the seclusion into spiritual elevation: they are for the one whose limbs are restrained from faults, whose heart is restrained from heedlessness, whose secret is restrained from all cohabitation with likes and peers. The seclusion of the houris figures the seclusion of the heart, kept for its Lord alone.

mots.racine: ح و ر / ق ص ر / خ ي م
mots.def: ḥūr: houris; maqṣūrāt: secluded; khiyām: tents.

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَانٌّ
versets.tr: lam yaṭmithhunna insun qablahum wa-lā jānnun
versets.trad: Untouched before them by human or jinn.
versets.commentaire: A reprise of the motif of inviolate purity. Qushayrī: none but the friend (al-walī) has approached them, none but he has possessed them. What is reserved is kept intact: the promised felicity is exclusive, granted to him alone for whom God destined it.

mots.ar: لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَانٌّ
mots.tr: lam yaṭmithhunna insun qablahum wa-lā jānn
mots.racine: ط م ث
mots.sens: untouched by human or jinn before

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَىٰ رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَعَبْقَرِيٍّ حِسَانٍ
versets.tr: muttakiʾīna ʿalā rafrafin khuḍrin wa-ʿabqariyyin ḥisānin
versets.trad: Reclining on green cushions and beautiful carpets.
versets.commentaire: The blessed recline on green cushions (rafraf) and beautiful wrought carpets (ʿabqarī). Qushayrī recalls that ʿabqarī denotes, among the Arabs, any finely embroidered cloth. The repose of Paradise is a completed repose: support, verdure, and beauty surround the elect on every side.

mots.racine: ر ف ر ف / خ ض ر
ع ب ق ر / ح س ن

versets.trad: So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?
versets.commentaire: The sūra's refrain, repeated thirty-one times, addresses the twofold audience of humans and jinn (hence the dual rabbikumā). Qushayrī: the ālāʾ are the favours — “by which of your Lord's favours do you deny?” Each repetition seals a grace and calls not to denial but to gratitude; the very hammering is a pedagogy of thankfulness, willing the heart to say yes to each gift.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by which of your Lord's favours

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, call a lie.

versets.ar: تَبَارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ
versets.tr: tabāraka smu rabbika dhī l-jalāli wa-l-ikrāmi
versets.trad: Blessed be the Name of your Lord, full of Majesty and Munificence.
versets.commentaire: The sūra, opened on the Name al-Raḥmān, is sealed on the blessing of the Lord's Name, full of Majesty and Munificence. The two poles — jalāl (the majesty that overwhelms) and ikrām (the generosity that fills) — sum up the whole journey: from the fear that dreads the Majesty to the grace that receives the Munificence. The Name that taught all things is the Name that deserves, in return, all blessing.

mots.racine: ب ر ك / س م و
mots.sens: blessed be the Name of your Lord
mots.def: tabāraka: to be a source of blessing.

mots.racine: ج ل ل / ك ر م
